```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

implementation","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

A ZMQ inproc pair is an area of memory shared between two threads and wrapped to look like ZMQ sockets.

> I see that you use AnyIO memory object streams in the sub-shell manager, could you explain your use of them, in particular if they are used for inter-thread communication?

Here is where they are created in the source code which includes some explanation:
```python
# anyio memory object stream for async queue-like communication between tasks.
# Used by _create_subshell to tell listen_from_subshells to spawn a new task.
self._send_stream, self._receive_stream = create_memory_object_stream[str]()
```
The communication is between two different async tasks running in the same thread.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipykernel/issues/comments/2388977572/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

implementation","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.


is the implementation of the kernel subshells JEP (jupyter/enhancement-proposals#91). It follows the latest commit ([1f1ad3d](https://github.com/jupyter/enhancement-proposals/pull/91/commits/1f1ad3d43f24299efb7cc7879cefeee57277906c)) with the addition of a `%subshell` magic command that is useful for debugging. To try this out I have a [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) that talks to this branch and is most easily tried out using https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/jep91_demo?urlpath=lab; once the mybinder instance has started, open the `subshell_demo_notebook.ipynb` and follow the instructions therein.

The idea is that this is mergeable as it is now, it is backward compatible in that it does not break any existing use of `ipykernel`  (subject to CI confirmation). There are some ramifications of the protocol additions (outlined below) that will need addressing eventually, but I consider these future work that can be in separate PRs. 

# Outline of changes
1. The parent subshell (i.e. the main shell) runs in the main thread.
2. Each new subshell runs in a separate thread.
3. There is a new thread that deals with all communication on the shell channel, previously this was performed in the main thread.
4. Communication between the shell channel thread and other threads is performed using ZMQ inproc pair sockets, which are essentially shared memory and avoid the use of thread synchronisation primitives.
5. Incoming shell messages are handled by the shell channel thread which extracts the `subshell_id` from the message and passes it on to the correct subshell.
6. Subshells are created and deleted via messages sent on the control channel. These are passed to the shell channel thread via inproc pair sockets so that the `SubshellManager` in the shell channel thread is responsible for subshell lifetimes.

# Example scenario

Here is an example of the communication between threads when running a long task in the parent subshell (main thread) and whilst this is running a child subshell is created, used, and deleted.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant client as Client
    participant control as Control thread
    participant shell as Shell channel thread
    participant main as Main thread

    client->>+shell: Execute request (main shell)
    shell->>-main: Execute request (inproc)
    activate main

    client->>+control: Create subshell request
    control->>-shell: Create subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    create participant subshell as Subshell thread
    shell-->>subshell: Create subshell thread

    shell->>control: Create subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Create subshell reply

    client->>+shell: Execute request (subshell)
    shell->>-subshell: Execute request (inproc)
    activate subshell

    subshell->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate subshell
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (subshell)

    client->>+control: Delete subshell request
    control->>-shell: Delete subshell request (inproc)
    activate shell
    destroy subshell
    shell-->>subshell: Delete subshell thread

    shell->>control: Delete subshell reply (inproc)
    deactivate shell
    activate control
    control->>-client: Delete subshell reply

    main->>shell: Execute reply (inproc)
    deactivate main
    activate shell
    shell->>-client: Execute reply (main shell)
```

# Future work

## ipykernel

1. Shell channel thread deserialises ~the whole~ some of the message to get the `subshell_id`. Ideally it would only deserialise the header.  May need changes in Jupyter Client.
2. Signalling a subshell to stop uses a `threading.Event` following the existing `anyio` implementation which requires an extra thread per `Event`. It would be nice if this could be changed so a subshell is a single thread not two.
3. Execution count. Should either be a separate count per subshell or a single count for a kernel. Needs a decision and changes in IPython as is currently not atomic.
4. History. Related to item 2 above.
5. `input()` on more than one subshell at the same time run but do not store correctly.
6. Debugger use needs investigating.
7. Busy/idle status needs investigating. Should there, as now, be separate status for each subshell, or the concept of kernel (i.e. any subshell) busy status? This issue is much wider than subshells as it includes status of the control channel, and how Jupyter Server should track status (jupyter-server/jupyter_server#1429).
8. Use of display hooks for e.g. Matplotlib. Should these be on the parent subshell, or child subshells too?

## JupyterLab

The [JupyterLab branch](https://github.com/ianthomas23/jupyterlab/tree/jep91_demo) I am using to demo this isn't really intended to be merged. But if it was, it needs:
1. Check `kernel_info` to see if subshells are supported.
2. Delete subshell when close a subshell's `ConsolePanel`.
3. Report subshell IDs in tree view?
4. Display of subshell busy/idle status.

of this problem shows that it is due to a couple of side-effects that are not usually an issue, but are when using `~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_kernel_config.py` to force `inline` as the backend. Since Matplotlib 3.9.0 we have moved IPython's backend handling into Matplotlib where it rightly belongs, and removed as much of the special-casing of the `inline` backend as possible. For a non-builtin backend (i.e. one that does not ship with Matplotlib) we identify the GUI loop for the backend by loading the backend module here https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/5a51fa219cffbd3744e0e9f161bd7655c08c7515/lib/matplotlib/backends/registry.py#L120 and checking its `required_interactive_framework`. The first unfortunate side-effect is that when we load the `inline` backend module it auto-runs some code here: https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/blob/90034cf74d7e5a6dea54b9d8f148427450354c45/matplotlib_inline/backend_inline.py#L228 This calls `matplotlib.get_backend()`. This unfortunately isn't a direct lookup in `rcParams` as there is another side-effect here: https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/5a51fa219cffbd3744e0e9f161bd7655c08c7515/lib/matplotlib/__init__.py#L793-L797 which in this particular scenario causes a `switch_backend` to the default backend for the OS in question, so `macosx` here. So this particular attempt to force use of the `inline` backend ends up using `macosx` instead. One solution would be to remove the automatic call of `_enable_matplotlib_integration` in matplotlib-inline. However, this breaks a whole bunch of Jupyter-derived libraries that depend on this behaviour, and I do not want to make such a change that could be detrimental to many users for the sake of fixing an issue that affects almost nobody. An alternative is to replace in matplotlib-inline the ```python backend = get_backend() ``` call with ```python backend = rcParams._get("backend") ``` as this avoids the side-effect of calling `switch_backend`. According to the `RcParams._get` docstring: https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/5a51fa219cffbd3744e0e9f161bd7655c08c7515/lib/matplotlib/__init__.py#L700-L713 this is an acceptable thing for me to do even though `_get` is private. Before writing a PR with this change I wanted to give other devs a chance to express an opinion for or against this change.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2402411376/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Bug summary

When running in debug mode using `matplotlib >= 3.9`, plots do not appear when called in the source code or in a debug console. Plots appear properly when executing source code outside of the debugger, so this may be an issue with interactive mode. I ran into these issues on both MacOS and Windows.

### Code for reproduction

```Python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure()
plt.plot(range(10))
plt.show()
```


### Actual outcome

Plots are displayed properly when running the source code outside of the debugger, but plots are not shown when debugging.

### Expected outcome

Figures created in source code or the debug console are shown once a breakpoint is hit. These figures should be able to be modified using commands in the debug console.

### Additional information

From my testing, plots are displayed properly in version 3.8 but not version 3.9. I have tried this in `conda` and `venv` and found that the only solution was downgrading to version 3.8.

### Operating system

Windows, OS/X

### Matplotlib Version

3.9.0

### Matplotlib Backend

maxosx (OS/X), tkagg (Windows)

### Python version

3.10.15

### Jupyter version

_No response_

### Installation

Bug summary

Matplotlib 3.9.0, PySide2 with Qt5 5.15.2, Python 3.11.9, IPython 8.25.0 (or jupter qtconsole 5.5.2)

The attached code leaves the plot in a non-interative state.

The blank figure comes up correctly with the plt.figure() call.  The plot draws correctly at fig.canvas.flush_events(). the final state is not interactive.

### Code for reproduction

```Python
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Qt5Agg')

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x=np.arange(10)
y=x*x

plt.ion()

fig=plt.figure()
ax=fig.add_subplot(111)
line,=ax.plot(x,y)

fig.canvas.draw()
fig.canvas.flush_events()
```


### Actual outcome

Plot is not responsive but is displayed correctly.



### Expected outcome

Should be able to interact with the plot, including selection of toolbar buttons, zooming etc.

### Additional information

The submission is a simplified use-case of a more complex applicaion under development. The plots in the application used to be left in an interactive state once the applications simulation completes execution.

### Operating system

Ubuntu 20.04

### Matplotlib Version

3.9.0

### Matplotlib Backend

Qt5Agg

### Python version

3.11.9

### Jupyter version

IPython 8.25.0 or jupyter qtconsole 5.5.2

### Installation

is an issue reported in Matplotlib (matplotlib/matplotlib#28855) in which use of a config file `~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_kernel_config.py` containing `c.InteractiveShellApp.matplotlib = 'inline'` results in the default Matplotlib backend being enabled rather than `matplotlib-inline`. I think this has occurred historically in some situations (such as #25) but is now more likely given the recent movement of the backend-handling code from IPython to Matplotlib.\n\nMy analysis (https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/28855#issuecomment-2402411376) is that it is a simple fix in matplotlib-inline to allow us to check what the current Matplotlib backend is without automatically enabling the defaults backend if none has yet been specified. I will submit a PR with this fix shortly, when I have performed some comprehensive manual testing that it does not break some other current use case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/37/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/37/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":38,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pulls/38","id":2130064293,"node_id":"PR_kwDOFCbR0c5-9jOl","html_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38","diff_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/38","number":38,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Do not enable default Matplotlib backend when checking what the backend is","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Fixes #37 and #25.

When importing `backend_inline` the function `_enable_matplotlib_integration()` is automatically called and this checks what the current Matplotlib backend is. Previously calling `matplotlib.get_backend()` if no backend was currently set would as a side-effect force it to be the default backend for that OS rather than leaving it unset. Changing it to `matplotlib.rcParams._get("backend")` avoids the side-effect thus fixing the problem.

Although `rcParams._get` is a private function this is in fact the correct function to use as explained in the Matplotlib source code:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/eb812a8e479d0bcf43a30232c31afec78963ed53/lib/matplotlib/__init__.py#L698-L713

To reproduce the original problem and confirm this PR fixes it use the following on the `main` branch and this PR branch:

1. Create file `~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_kernel_config.py` containing the single line `c.InteractiveShellApp.matplotlib = 'inline'`.
2. Use either `jupyter lab` or `jupyter qtconsole` and run the following code: `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; plt.plot([1,3,2])`

On `main` branch this uses the wrong Matplotlib backend and on this PR branch it used the correct `inline` backend.

I have manually tested other use cases of using Matplotlib in `ipython` and `jupyter` and it looks like this fix does not introduce any new is","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-17T12:25:30Z","updated_at":"2024-10-17T13:11:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pulls/38","html_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38","diff_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Fixes #37 and #25.

When importing `backend_inline` the function `_enable_matplotlib_integration()` is automatically called and this checks what the current Matplotlib backend is. Previously calling `matplotlib.get_backend()` if no backend was currently set would as a side-effect force it to be the default backend for that OS rather than leaving it unset. Changing it to `matplotlib.rcParams._get("backend")` avoids the side-effect thus fixing the problem.

Although `rcParams._get` is a private function this is in fact the correct function to use as explained in the Matplotlib source code:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/eb812a8e479d0bcf43a30232c31afec78963ed53/lib/matplotlib/__init__.py#L698-L713

To reproduce the original problem and confirm this PR fixes it use the following on the `main` branch and this PR branch:

1. Create file `~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_kernel_config.py` containing the single line `c.InteractiveShellApp.matplotlib = 'inline'`.
2. Use either `jupyter lab` or `jupyter qtconsole` and run the following code: `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; plt.plot([1,3,2])`

On `main` branch this uses the wrong Matplotlib backend and on this PR branch it used the correct `inline` backend.

I have manually tested other use cases of using Matplotlib in `ipython` and `jupyter` and it looks like this fix does not introduce any new problems.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/38/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/38/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/comments/2419509116","html_url":"https://github.com/ipython/matplotlib-inline/pull/38#issuecomment-2419509116","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/matplotlib-inline/issues/38","id":2419509116,"node_id":"IC_kwDOFCbR0c6QNsd8","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-17T13:11:06Z","updated_at":"2024-10-17T13:11:06Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The test image changes are on the `main` branch rather than this PR; A `StackColorMapper` for displaying RGB[A] images","user":{"login":"shaperilio","id":26465923,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2NDY1OTIz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26465923?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio","html_url":"https://github.com/shaperilio","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/shaperilio/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":91228996,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTIyODk5Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/labels/type:%20discussion","name":"type: discussion","color":"2659A0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-10-17T15:07:13Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T16:12:56Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Problem description

In #1699 a desire was expressed to display RGBA images with shape (M, N, 3|4), e.g. directly out of `imread` (`matplotlib`). Using `image_rgba` works, but requires "bitpacking" (e.g. see [here](https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/issues/1699#issuecomment-2417446913)) and then you can't get a hover tooltip with RGBA values out of the box (you just get the 32-bit value).

I explored the possibility of using `image_stack` instead, and it appears that the only thing missing is a `StackColorMapper` implementation that has an optional user-set minimum *and* maximum value.

The MRE below makes a plot with `WeightedStackColorMapper`, which does not produce a proper result; a weighted average makes an image with R, G, B = (1, 1, 1) show up as 33% grey. But it shows other things working: the hover tooltip shows the R, G, B tuple out of the box, and it would solve #1699 because no bit-packing is necessary to display a color image.

```python
import numpy

from bokeh.plotting import figure, output_file, show, save
from bokeh.models import WeightedStackColorMapper, LinearColorMapper
from bokeh.palettes import varying_alpha_palette

output_file('/tmp/plot.html)
w, h = 64, 64
p = figure(width=w * 16, height=h * 16,
           tools='hover', tooltips=[
               ('X, Y', '$x, $y'),
               ('R, G, B', '@image'),
           ])

# We need to define an alpha mapper but it doesn't actually matter:
# 1. The RGB values returned are ignored by the `WeightedStackColorMapper`.
# 2. The alpha values are unused because we have global alpha set to 1.0 (the
#    default).
alpha_mapper = LinearColorMapper(palette=varying_alpha_palette(color='white',
                                                               n=2,
                                                               start_alpha=0,
                                                               end_alpha=255))
cm = WeightedStackColorMapper(palette=['#FF0000', '#00FF00', '#0000FF'],
                              stack_labels=['R', 'G', 'B'],  # NB: don't know how to use these
                              alpha_mapper=alpha_mapper)


# *** test; should be white
R = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
G = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
B = numpy.ones((h, w), dtype=numpy.uint8)
image = [numpy.stack((R, G, B), axis=-1)]
p.image_stack(image=image,
              x=0, y=0,  # origin location of image
              dw=w, dh=h,  # extent of image
              color_mapper=cm,
              )
# *** ends up grey. `WeightedStackColorMapper` does a "weighted sum", i.e. for
# each pixel, the "total" = R+G+B+A, and each color is computed as color /
# total. So for all white, you get 1 / 3 = 33% grey.
show(p)
```

### Feature description

I propose a new `StackColorMapper` implementation; let's call it `RGBAStackColorMapper` for the purpose of discussion. If it were written in Python, it may look something like this:

```python
class RGBAStackColorMapper(StackColorMapper):
    def __init__(self,
                 min_color: float | None = None,
                 max_color: float | None = None,
                 palette: Sequence[str] | None = None
                 ) -> None:
        """
        Maps the values in a stack of shape (H, W, C) into RGBA values.

        Parameters
        ----------
        min_color: float | None
            Minimum value. If `None`, the minimum value in the stack is used.

        max_color: float | None
            Maximum value. If `None`, the maximum value in the stack is used.

        palette: Sequence[str]
            Sequence of colors, one for each channel in the stack. These colors
            represent the maximum value for that channel, i.e. the color
            displayed when a pixel has `max_color` for that channel. Must be
            length 3.
        """
        self._min_color = min_color
        self._max_color = max_color
        if palette is None:
            palette = ['#FF0000', '#00FF00', '#0000FF']
        self._palette = palette
        assert len(self._palette) == 3

    def map_to_RGBA(self, value: Sequence[float]) -> tuple[int, int, int, int]:
        """
        Maps a value to an RGBA value.

        Parameters
        ----------
        value: Sequence[float]
            The values of the stack at a particular pixel

        Returns
        -------
        Tuple[int, int, int, int]
            The RGBA value.
        """
        # Must have one color for each channel, possibly with alpha
        assert len(value) in (3, 4)

        # Clip values to the min and mx. (Assume `min_color` and `max_color`
        # have already been set to the stack's min and max if they were passed
        # in as `None`.)
        n = self._min_color
        x = self._max_color
        value = [min(max(v, n), x) for v in value]
        # TODO: special handling when x==n
        R = int((value[0] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        G = int((value[1] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        B = int((value[2] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        if len(value) == 4:
            A = int((value[3] - n) / (x - n) * 255)
        else:
            A = 255
        return R, G, B, A
```

These are the features of this mapper:
1. `min_color` and `max_color` can be explicitly set (e.g. to 0 and 255 to show unaltered images loaded from disk). If not set, the min/max of the stack is used, which would have the effect of normalizing the image. The caller can also set explicit values (e.g. 64, 200) and values will be clipped (like the levels tool in Photoshop).
2. `palette` by default is simply RGB. Setting it allows other "colormaps" to be used.
3. There's no explicit setting of an alpha mapper. If the stack has 4 channels, the last one is assumed to be alpha. (This would require callers to shuffle their data around if it's not in RGBA channel order on the last dim, but I think that's minor.) The idea here is that, when working with `WeightedStackColorMapper` in the MRE above, I had to create an alpha mapper that was never used and thus could be basically anything, and I want to avoid that here.

For the MRE, the use would be as follows:
```python
cm = RGBAStackColorMapper(min_color=0, max_color=1)
```

### Potential alternatives

I could not reproduce the output of `image_rgba` with `image_stack` and `WeightedStackColorMapper`. I did not explore beyond that.

If people are finding it difficult getting data in the format that `image_rgba` expects and/or to display the individual color components when hovering then I would rather add some extra helper functions to make these tasks easier.

In general we'd expect to eventually have other classes derived from `StackColorMapper`. The one and only derived class `WeightedColorMapper` has one color per 2D data array and dynamically determines the weights for combining these colors based on the underlying data. PR summary

MSVC on the Azure/AppVeyor builds currently produce a few warnings. These are due to some lax input types, or otherwise because of differing type sizes on Windows. The values are generally not in the range where it would matter, so we can make the casts explicit to silence the warnings.

For the incorrect types, the old types are deprecated, but still accepted.

This is based on #28842 at the moment due to conflicting changes, but the last four commits are the relevant ones here.

MSVC on the Azure/AppVeyor builds currently produce a few warnings. These are due to some lax input types, or otherwise because of differing type sizes on Windows. The values are generally not in the range where it would matter, so we can make the casts explicit to silence the warnings.

For the incorrect types, the old types are deprecated, but still accepted.

This is based on #28842 at the moment due to conflicting changes, but the last four commits are the relevant ones here.

is currently targeting `ipykernel` 7.0, with support for [AnyIO](https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/pull/1079).

We have to address the following before releasing:

- [x] `inprocess` tests are failing after #1079, and are currently skipped
- [ ] `jupyter_client` tests are failing.  We will need to make a release that supports both `ipykernel` 6.0 and 7.0
- [ ] `qtconsole`tests are failing.  We will need to make a release that supports both `ipykernel` 6.0 and 7.0, or a release that pins to (`<7.0`).

- [x] Pin to (`<7.0`) in [metakernel](https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/) for now - https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/issues/275

{"action":"opened","number":1285,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipykernel/pulls/1285","id":2145833232,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAgGK785_5tEQ","html_url":"https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/pull/1285","diff_url":"https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/pull/1285.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/ipython/ipykernel/pull/1285.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/ipython/ipykernel/issues/1285","number":1285,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Improve robustness of subshell concurrency tests","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Improve robustness of subshell concurrency tests. This is difficult to do as the tests run reliably fine on my dev machine so I am looking at what could theoretically improve the situation and trying it out in CI.\r\n\r\nI have moved the `import time` to before starting the time-sensitive subshell code as this should be better than importing it in one subshell (taking an unknown amount of time) or importing it in both subshells at the same time (GIL contention?). If this is not sufficient I could add some threaded synchronisation to the start of the subshell test code to remove any timing variability in sending/receiving zmq minimum pybind11 to 2.13.2 as use of 2.13.1 can cause mutex problems in Python 3.13 free-threaded builds, see pybind/pybind11#5420.

It would be good to confirm exactly which version of pybind11 was used to build the matplotlib 3.9.2 cp313t wheels on PyPI. They are dated Aug 13, the same date that pybind11 2.13.2 and 2.13.3 were released. So the mpl wheels could potentially have used 2.13.1 which would be bad. The GHA run that built and uploaded the wheel is https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/actions/runs/10361240538/job/28683177700 but it is not verbose enough to including the pybind11 version used.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29021/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29021/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2439654732","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/pull/29021#issuecomment-2439654732","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29021","id":2439654732,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABUios6Rai1M","user":{"login":"ianthomas23","id":580326,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4MDMyNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/580326?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23","html_url":"https://github.com/ianthomas23","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ianthomas23/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-26T16:56:20Z","updated_at":"2024-10-26T16:56:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think the matplotlib 3.9.2 cp313t wheels (or at least the manylinux x86_64 one) were built using the problematic pybind11 2.13.1. If I create a new 3.13.0t virtual environment using pyenv and install the latest contourpy (1.3.0) and matplotlib (3.9.2) wheels and run some code that uses both matplotlib and contourpy:

```bash
$ PYTHON_GIL=0 python
Python 3.13.0 experimental free-threading build (main, Oct 26 2024, 16:31:15) [GCC 13.2.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>>> plt.contour([[1,2],[3,4]], levels=5)
Floating point exception (core dumped)
```
If I then install the latest matplotlib nightly wheel (3.10.0.dev900+g835d5125a3) which definitely uses a later pybind11 the above code works fine and I can `plt.show()` successfully.

But maybe the fact that the matplotlib 3.9.2 cp313t wheels are problematic is moot. We aren't fully claiming free-threaded support until there is a matplotlib release containing PR #28819 and a kiwisolver release with the equivalent (nucleic/kiwi#186). Until then if you try to import matplotlib in a free-threaded venv you get the warning that matplotlib/kiwisolver aren't claiming they can run safely without the GIL and you have to, at your own risk, use `PYTHON_GIL=0` or equivalent.

So given that matplotlib 3.10 is imminent and includes #28819 (and hopefully this PR) then I think we are OK to point users towards that release if they want free-threaded support. We can't really do a 3.9.3 release including #28819 as that would also need all of the recent pybind11 PRs which is not appropriate for a patch release.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2439654732/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

We currently have:

- `matplotlib.use`
- `matplotlib.get_backend`
- `pyplot.switch_backend`

Issues:

- The name `matplotlib.use` is really obscure. 
- The difference between `switch_backend` and `use` is not quite clear.

### Proposed fix

- Consider renaming `matplotlib.use` (and either discourace or long-term deprecate `use` (with a pending deprecation)).
- Check whether `use` and `switch_backend` can be unified. If not, at least improve the documentation.

---

Side note: The `force` parameter in `use` can be dropped.

```
matplotlib.use(..., force=True)
```
is equivalent to
```
try:
    matplotlib.use(...)
except ImportError:
    pass
```
It should be rare that users want to ignore that changing the backend fails. And if so, they can do it explicitly. We do not need to provide API for that. The only slight complication is that this the default behavior is force=True; i.e. dropping would change the default behavor. But we can address that in the course of "renaming": We simply provide a new function, e.g. `set_backend` with the new behavior.
